title: Welcome to Premed

objectives.statement: Describe the course journey from scientific inquiry and physical science through living systems, human systems, and integration.
Explain how at least two major subject areas contribute connected parts of a medicine-facing scientific question.
Describe what it would look like to explain, model, investigate, and integrate scientific ideas by course completion.

scenes.title: Why one question needs several sciences
scenes.contentHtml:
Why one question needs several sciences

Imagine reaching the top of three flights of stairs. Your pulse is loud, your breathing is quick, and one small question appears: what made all of that change together?

A good answer cannot stay inside one subject. You would need trustworthy observations. You would need to reason about movement, matter, and energy. Then you would need to connect molecules to cells, cells to organs, and organs to a whole person. This pattern of reasoning—from a question to a connected explanation—is the shape of this Premed course.

The course follows five linked questions:

  1. How do we know? Scientific inquiry, mathematics, statistics, and laboratory practice help you decide what to measure, how much confidence to place in a result, and which conclusions the evidence can support.
  2. What is moving or changing? Physics and chemistry help you reason about forces, fluids, electricity, waves, matter, and energy.
  3. What is a living system doing? Biology and genetics help explain how cells use genetic information, reproduce, vary, and change. Organic chemistry and biochemistry connect molecular structure and reactions to what cells do.
  4. How do the parts work together? Physiology, immunity, microbiology, behaviour, and society widen the view from molecules and cells to a person and a population.
  5. Does the explanation still work in a new problem? Integration work and the final capstone—a task that brings earlier work together—ask you to choose the ideas that fit, connect them, and say what the available evidence does and does not support.

The questions are in a useful order, but they are not separate boxes. Scientific inquiry runs through the entire course. Chemistry makes later biochemistry easier to understand. Cell biology gives physiology something to build on. Physics helps explain processes such as pressure, flow, electrical signalling, and imaging. Later medical study draws on all of these levels at once.

By the end, you should be able to do more than recognize scientific terms. You should be able to:

  • explain causes and consequences step by step;
  • make a model—a deliberately simpler version of a situation—use it to make a prediction, and explain its limits;
  • investigate a question with suitable measurements; and
  • combine several subjects in an unfamiliar problem.

Those abilities are preparation for further study. They are not a medical qualification or a promise of admission.

Start from what you already notice

Check
id check-starting-point

Choose an ordinary question about a living system. It might concern movement, sleep, infection, inheritance, food, or a laboratory measurement. Without looking anything up, name two kinds of science that might help answer it. Write one question each subject could address.

There is no score here and no expected science vocabulary. If the subject names are unfamiliar, borrow two of the five bold questions above. The useful first step is noticing that one question can be viewed at more than one level.

By the end of this lesson, you will be able to tell the story of the course, make at least two of its connections, and picture the work you are preparing to do. You are not being tested on chemistry, biology, or physics today.

Source Note
claims claim-course-journey, claim-subject-connections, claim-end-capabilities, claim-course-boundarysources source-premed-syllabus

The Premed syllabus describes this route and finish line in its opening promise, overview of course abilities, sequence at a glance, and welcome section. It describes this course; it does not define every route into medical study.

scenes.title: Map one question before answering it
scenes.contentHtml:
Map one question before answering it

You do not need to know exercise physiology to see how the subjects connect. Here is a complete example of choosing the right questions before you know the science well enough to answer them.

Worked Example
id worked-use-running-question

Task. A fictional learner records a quicker pulse and quicker breathing after a short run. Build a plan for explaining why the two observations belong in the same investigation. The task is to choose and connect the kinds of knowledge the question needs, not to diagnose anyone or explain the physiology in advance.

What is given? Two observations made before and after an activity.

What is unknown? The chain of causes connecting the activity to both changes, and whether the measurements are good enough to support an explanation.

Choose a useful course map. Begin with evidence because an explanation is only as good as the observations it must explain. Next ask about physical and molecular processes. Then build from cells to organs and the whole-body response. This map fits because the question crosses several levels.

Plan. Ask one useful question at each level, then state how the answers would connect.

  1. Begin with inquiry and measurement. Ask how pulse and breathing were measured, what was recorded before the run for comparison, and whether repeated measurements show the same pattern.
  2. Add physics and chemistry. Ask what is moving—such as air, blood, or electric charge—what makes it move, and how energy is carried, released, or used.
  3. Add biology and biochemistry. Ask what active cells need during activity and which cell processes meet those needs.
  4. Add physiology. Ask how breathing, circulation, nerves, and muscles could coordinate rather than treating each observation as a separate event.

Check the plan. Each subject should add a useful part to the same explanation. Their roles may overlap; the important thing is to state how each part connects to the next. A useful plan also names one possible result that would make you doubt or revise the explanation.

Interpretation. This map is not yet an explanation of exercise physiology. It shows why the course begins with evidence and foundations, then builds toward cells, systems, and integration. When the science has been learned, the same map can guide an explanation that is supported by evidence and honest about its limits.

The decisive choice was not a formula or a fact. It was deciding to treat the observations as parts of one question and then choosing a sensible order in which to connect them.

Before moving on, answer in one sentence: Which connection in the plan matters most, and what would be missing if you skipped it? More than one answer can be sound. A strong answer names both sides of the connection and the gap that would remain.

Source Note
claims claim-course-journey, claim-subject-connections, claim-course-boundarysources source-premed-syllabus

The syllabus places inquiry across the course and builds from physical and molecular foundations toward living systems, human systems, and integration. The running example and its questions were created for this lesson; they are not copied from the syllabus or offered as medical guidance.

scenes.title: Make the connections yourself
scenes.contentHtml:
Make the connections yourself

The first example supplied the map. Now you will do more of the work yourself: recall the route, repair an incomplete map, and choose a route through a new question.

Retrieve before you look back

Check
id check-five-questions

Without reopening the first scene, write the five guiding questions in your own words. Then name the thread that should run through every subject.

Make a real attempt before reading on.

Your answer may use different words. It should begin by asking how we know, then move through physical and chemical change, living cells and genetic information, and coordinated systems, people, and populations. It should end by asking whether the combined explanation works in a new problem. Scientific inquiry keeps returning: check measurements, reason from evidence, and notice the limits.

If you remembered only two or three parts, add the missing questions now. That repair is more useful than rereading the list without trying to recall it.

A subject list is not yet an explanation

Misconception
id misconception-subject-inventory

Consider this answer: “The course is mathematics, chemistry, biology, and physiology.”

Before reading further, say what the list leaves out.

The list tells us which subjects appear, but not what each subject helps explain or how one prepares you to use another. The same four labels could sit beside many unrelated questions.

Repair the answer by adding actions and connections: “Mathematics helps us judge measurements; chemistry helps us reason about molecular change; biology connects those changes to cells; physiology connects cells and organs in a whole-body response.” Now the reader can follow the work from one level to the next.

Recheck the idea with “chemistry and biochemistry.” Write one sentence that connects them instead of merely placing them side by side.

A useful reply might say that chemistry supplies ideas about molecular structure, interaction, and change that biochemistry uses to explain processes in living systems. Your sentence can differ if it makes a clear, accurate connection.

Practise with less support

Completion problem. Someone asks whether a change in genetic information could be connected to a change in movement. Complete these three lines without trying to answer the science:

  1. Start with evidence by asking …
  2. Connect two subject areas by asking how …
  3. Check the proposed connection by looking for …

Your route could connect genetics to molecular and cell biology, then connect cells to muscles, nerves, or movement. Physics may also help if the question turns to force or motion. The point is not to include every subject. The point is to choose the subjects that change what you can ask or explain.

Try a new kind of question. Imagine a headline that says, “People who use a health app sleep better, so the app improves sleep.” Map a course route for investigating the claim. Name the first question you would ask, two subject areas that could contribute, the connection between them, and one result that would make you doubt the claim.

After you answer, check your route:

  • Did you begin by asking what was measured and whether the headline claims more than the evidence supports?
  • Did each chosen subject add a question or method, rather than serving as a label?
  • Did you connect behaviour or biology to evidence and quantitative reasoning?
  • Did you name a result that could make you revise the explanation?

If you wrote only a list, add a verb and a connection to every subject. If you included almost every subject, remove any one that does not change the investigation. Then try again with a different non-personal question.

Source Note
claims claim-course-journey, claim-subject-connections, claim-end-capabilitiessources source-premed-syllabus

The syllabus describes the course as connected work in explanation, evidence, modelling, investigation, and integration. The genetic-information and health-app questions are fictional tasks created for this welcome; they make no claim about a real person or study.

scenes.title: Use the map, then picture the finish line
scenes.contentHtml:
Use the map, then picture the finish line

The destination is not a shelf full of separate facts. It is the ability to use scientific ideas together and to show how you reached a conclusion.

By course completion, four verbs should describe real work you can do:

  • Explain. Trace causes and consequences step by step, connect each step, and distinguish what the evidence supports from what it does not.
  • Model. Make a deliberately simpler version of a situation, use it to predict or interpret something, and state what the model leaves out.
  • Investigate. Turn a question into measurements or observations, choose a fair comparison, keep a usable record, and judge the limits of the result.
  • Integrate. Choose ideas from more than one subject and connect molecules, cells, systems, people, or populations when the question requires it.

These abilities make the earlier subject journey useful. Inquiry keeps the evidence honest. Mathematics makes patterns and uncertainty clearer. Chemistry and physics provide models of matter, energy, forces, and movement. Biology and biochemistry connect those models to living systems. Human sciences and the final integration work ask how the pieces act together.

Completing the course does not qualify you to diagnose or treat anyone. It is preparation for later study, not a degree, academic credit, a medical qualification, or a guarantee of admission.

Show that you have the map

Answer these three prompts without looking back. They ask you to orient yourself, not to recall scientific facts.

  1. Describe the journey. In a short paragraph, describe at least four stages of the course and explain how one stage prepares you for another.
  2. Connect the subjects. A report claims that a change in a protein causes a change in movement. Without deciding whether the claim is true, map at least three course lenses that could investigate it. Explain the connection between each lens and name evidence that could make you revise the route.
  3. Picture the finish line. Describe what it would look like to explain, model, investigate, and integrate a scientific question. Then name two things that completing this course would not promise.

After you submit, read the feedback for each answer and find the missing part of your map. On a retry, use a different connection or a different fictional question instead of memorising the sample answer.

Keep the map alive

Return to the five guiding questions when you begin chemistry, biology, physiology, and the final integration work. Ask: What can this subject explain now? Which earlier idea does it use? What evidence would connect the two? Rebuilding one connection after a delay will tell you more than simply recognizing the course list.

Recovery route

If the subject journey still feels like a long list, reduce it to three moves:

  1. Check the evidence.
  2. Connect small-scale processes to cells and systems.
  3. Test the combined explanation in a new problem.

Return to the worked running example and label those three moves. Then choose one new question and repeat them. Once that shorter route is clear, add the five detailed questions from the opening scene.

If a subject name is unfamiliar, you do not need to study that subject before finishing this welcome. Use the ordinary-language question beside it and continue.

Accessibility and alternatives

This lesson does not require exercise, personal health measurements, or private information. Every scenario is fictional. You may replace any scenario with a non-personal question that is easier or safer for you to use.

You can answer in sentences, bullets, a spoken response, or a text-only outline. Keep the connections in a clear order and name them in words; no meaning depends on colour, a diagram, dragging, sound, or a timed response.

Source Note
claims claim-course-journey, claim-subject-connections, claim-end-capabilities, claim-course-boundarysources source-premed-syllabus

The syllabus defines this course's sequence, finish line, and educational boundary. The questions above and the shorter recovery path were created for this welcome.

aiAssistance.disclosure: Codex drafted the lesson's original prose, learning activities, assessment, and structured records under the accountable principal named above. The draft has not received independent review or adjudication.

items.prompt: In 120–180 words, describe at least four stages of the Premed subject journey and explain how one stage prepares you to use another. Use ordinary language rather than a list of course titles.

answer.correct: A complete response moves from inquiry and evidence through physical and molecular foundations to living systems, human systems, and integration, and explains at least one connection between stages.
answer.summary: Describe a connected journey, not an inventory of subjects.
answer.reasoning: The outcome asks for orientation to the course as a sequence of connected questions. A strong response names at least four meaningful stages, gives each stage an instructional job, and explains how an idea or method from one stage becomes useful in another. Exact labels and order may vary when the connection remains faithful to the syllabus route.
acceptableResponses: Inquiry and quantitative reasoning establish how to measure and judge evidence; physics and chemistry explain physical and molecular change; biology and biochemistry connect those changes to living cells; physiology and related fields connect cells and organs; integration uses several levels in one new problem.
A route that begins with evidence, builds through matter and energy to cells and systems, and ends with integrated use, provided it explains at least one preparation link.
commonErrors: Listing subjects without saying what each contributes. Add one useful question or action to every subject named.
Describing the route as unrelated blocks. Add a sentence showing how an earlier idea becomes useful later.
Treating the welcome as a science test. Return to the five ordinary-language questions; no scientific fact recall is required.

items.prompt: A report claims that a change in a protein causes a change in movement. Without deciding whether the claim is true, map at least three course lenses that could investigate it. Explain how the lenses connect and name one possible result that would make you revise the route.
items.stimulus: Treat the report as fictional. You are mapping questions and evidence, not diagnosing a person or supplying the biological answer.

answer.correct: A complete response begins with evidence, connects protein or molecular questions to cells and then to movement or whole-system questions, and identifies a result that would weaken or redirect the proposed chain.
answer.summary: Choose connected lenses and leave room for the evidence to change the route.
answer.reasoning: The task does not require protein or movement science. It tests whether the learner can select inquiry plus relevant molecular, cellular, system, or physical lenses; state how an answer at one level would inform the next; and avoid treating the fictional causal claim as established.
acceptableResponses: Start by checking how protein change and movement were measured. Use biochemistry to ask what the protein does, cell biology to ask what changes in a cell, and physiology or physics to ask how that could appear as movement. Revise the route if the movement difference remains when the protein measure does not change, or if another factor better explains both.
Any route with at least three justified lenses, explicit connections, an evidence check, and a stated reason to revise.
commonErrors: Accepting the causal wording before checking the evidence. Begin with how the two changes were measured and compared.
Naming three subjects but not connecting them. State what answer passes from one level to the next.
Including every subject regardless of usefulness. Remove any lens that does not change the questions, evidence, or explanation.
Giving a biological answer instead of a course map. Return to what each lens would ask; the lesson has not taught the mechanism.

items.prompt: Describe what it would look like to explain, model, investigate, and integrate a scientific question by course completion. Then name two things that completing this course would not promise.

answer.correct: Explain means trace connected causes; model means use a deliberate simplification and state its limits; investigate means gather and judge suitable evidence; integrate means connect useful ideas across subjects. Completion does not confer clinical authority and does not guarantee admission, a degree, or academic credit.
answer.summary: Make each capability observable and keep the educational boundary clear.
answer.reasoning: The four verbs describe performances rather than exposure to content. A correct response says what a learner would do and includes two explicit boundaries so that preparation for later study is not confused with a credential or admission promise.
acceptableResponses: Equivalent plain-language descriptions of all four performances, plus any two of: no medical qualification, no clinical authority, no admission guarantee, no degree, and no academic credit.
commonErrors: Defining the finish line as remembering facts. Replace each noun with an action the learner could demonstrate.
Using 'understand' for all four capabilities. Give a different observable action for explain, model, investigate, and integrate.
Omitting the boundary. Add two things the course does not award or guarantee.

criteria.description: Names at least four meaningful stages or guiding questions in the course journey.
Explains how one earlier stage prepares the learner to use a later stage.
Presents a connected route in clear language rather than only listing subjects.
Selects inquiry and at least two other lenses that would change the questions or evidence considered.
Explains how the lenses connect across molecular, cellular, system, or physical levels.
Names a possible result that would weaken or redirect the proposed route.
Describes observable actions for explaining, modelling, investigating, and integrating.
Names two credentials, permissions, or admissions outcomes that course completion does not provide.

sources.title: Premed: Foundations for Medical Study
sources.locator: Course promise; sections 5 and 8; section 9, WEL-000 Welcome to Premed

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Only course-specific facts and curriculum structure were used. The lesson's wording, organization, examples, and assessment are independently created; CONTENT-LICENSE.md separately licenses course content under CC BY 4.0.

agentAccess.notes: The source was read from the user-supplied local checkout at the archived commit. The repository content license covers course/ under CC BY 4.0 and states no source-specific agent-ingestion restriction. No login, paywall, access control, scraping, or remote ingestion route was used.

claims.statement: EmbeddedKnowledge Premed begins with inquiry and quantitative reasoning, develops physical, chemical, biological, and biochemical foundations, then moves into human systems, medicine-facing integration, pathway work, and a capstone.
claims.scope: The planned sequence of EmbeddedKnowledge Premed curriculum version 1.1 candidate.
claims.uncertainty: The cited syllabus is a candidate version and the planned sequence may change in a later curriculum revision.

claims.statement: The course is designed so that inquiry and quantitative reasoning continue across subjects, while physical and molecular sciences support later explanations of cells, human systems, and integrated medicine-facing problems.
claims.scope: The curricular relationships and medicine-facing purpose declared by the EmbeddedKnowledge Premed syllabus, not a claim that every medical program uses the same prerequisite sequence.
claims.uncertainty: The lesson simplifies a large curriculum into five guiding questions; individual modules have more detailed and sometimes concurrent relationships.

claims.statement: The Premed finish line includes explaining, modelling, investigating, and integrating science with evidence, limitations, and cross-domain reasoning rather than merely recognizing terms.
claims.scope: The program-level capabilities and Welcome module destination stated for EmbeddedKnowledge Premed curriculum version 1.1 candidate.
claims.uncertainty: This orientation previews the capabilities; it does not itself demonstrate that a learner has achieved them.

claims.statement: Completing EmbeddedKnowledge Premed is educational preparation and does not confer a university degree, academic credit, medical qualification, clinical authority, or an admission guarantee.
claims.scope: The credential and safety boundary of EmbeddedKnowledge Premed.

terms.definition: Observations, measurements, or other information used to support, challenge, or revise an explanation.
The process of turning a question into observations or measurements, reasoning from the evidence, and revising the explanation when needed.
A deliberately simpler version of a situation used to explain, predict, or test something, with limits that must be stated.
Choosing and connecting useful ideas from more than one subject or level of explanation for the same question.
A final task in which a learner combines earlier knowledge and methods to address a substantial new problem.

attributionHtml:
Attribution

Lesson content

“Welcome to Premed” (PREM-WEL-001), version 0.1.0, was created for EmbeddedKnowledge under the accountable principal VSB (github:VSBDev). The GitHub principal was verified with the authenticated GitHub API on 20 July 2026.

The original lesson prose, fictional examples, practice, assessment, and structured records in this directory are offered under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International license (CC BY 4.0).

No third-party media, quotations, adapted expression, or datasets are included. The source registry records a facts-only use of the EmbeddedKnowledge Premed syllabus. All learner-facing wording, organization, examples, and questions were independently created for this lesson.

Agent assistance

Material agent assistance was used for authoring:

  • System: Codex
  • Provider: OpenAI
  • Model: GPT-5.6-Sol
  • Version: Codex CLI 0.144.6
  • Run ID: author-codex-gpt56sol-20260720-5153dfd4-fb9a-48a4-afb5-8a4d30ada0e6
  • Material-instructions digest: sha256:f19b2775d6372ed10743a8921fb17fdcca348ef6609230d851ad9ecbd25ab767

The digest covers a UTF-8 bundle made on the clean base commit afc8b980e1181d172b0954b7b46bfcdd32d0db85. Each part is preceded by a line of the form --- name ---. The exact order is: the task-specific user brief with one terminating line feed; AGENTS.md; llms.txt; CONTRIBUTING.md; .agents/skills/author-embeddedknowledge-lesson/SKILL.md; CONTENT-STANDARD.md; FORMAT.md; lessons/README.md; RIGHTS-POLICY.md; CONTENT-LICENSE.md; COLLABORATION.md; site/agent/contribution.json; site/agent/quorum-policy.json; and the lesson, assessment, references, claims, and glossary schemas under site/schemas/. The digest does not claim to cover hidden provider instructions that cannot be exported.

The accountable principal remains responsible for the contribution. This draft has no independent reviews or adjudication and makes no claim of publication or approval.
